06 Assigning Accountability - YouTube.mp4
This step is the fun part, in my opinion. This is when we're going to fill out the Critical Accountability Bullets, or CABs as we like to call them, for each member of the team. At this point, if you haven't already done so, you'll need to gather up all your value engines because they are going to serve as the foundation for defining both the work that needs to be done and who is uniquely responsible for doing that work. But before we dive into assigning your company's Critical Accountability Bullets, we should first define exactly what a CAB is and how it differs from other frameworks you may be familiar with, such as OKRs, KRAs, and even the humble job description.
The most important distinction between Critical Accountability Bullets is that they are defined by the value engine and not the role or the person doing the role. Here's what I mean. When it comes to clarifying roles and responsibilities, most companies go with the tried-and-true job description. Now, as the name implies, a job description simply describes the work that someone does The problem with job descriptions is that they are typically written before the team member is even hired, meaning they function more like an advertisement for the job than an actual description of the job. Also, job descriptions rarely, if ever, get updated, and over time, team members can feel lost and unclear about the role they occupy and what success looks like for that role.
So while job descriptions are certainly better than nothing, for decades, business leaders have acknowledged that they are incomplete, which is why more developed companies implement what are known as OKRs and KRAs. OKR is short for Objective and Key Results, and KRA is short for Key Results Area. When you combine a specific objective with a key results area, then congratulations, you have the basis for an Objective and Key Result, or OKR for short. Okay, are you sufficiently confused yet? Look, if you find all these acronyms overwhelming, you're not alone. Some of the most famous and best-selling business books of all time, from High Output Management to Measure What Matters, talk about OKRs and KRAs, but experts still argue about exactly what they mean and exactly how to define them.
And if you're confused, just imagine how your team is going to feel when you ask them to stop what they're doing so that they can define the OKRs and KRAs for their role If they manage to complete the task, you're likely to wind up with some corporate-y nonsense phrases like, ensure customer satisfaction, implement cross-departmental alignment. But let me be clear, OKRs and KRAs can work. In fact, they have worked for companies like Intel, Google, Twitter, Microsoft, and Uber, and last I checked, the leaders of those companies know a thing or two about scaling businesses. Oh yeah, and the person generally credited with creating the OKR-KRA framework is none other than Andy Grove, the former CEO of Intel, who quite literally wrote the book on creating high-output teams.
So I'm not going to be the guy that claims that Andy Grove was wrong. What I will say, though, is that in my not-so-humble opinion, these frameworks still miss the mark in two critical areas. First, they're just too complicated for most small and even medium-sized businesses to implement effectively. The time and attention required to do it right simply isn't feasible for most businesses, nor is it even helpful in a fast-growing, constantly changing entrepreneurial organization. Second, these frameworks are almost always top-down, meaning they start with the company goals and objectives, and then filter down to the teams, and then to the individual. Said another way, they are company-centric and role-centric rather than value-centric. OK, so if job descriptions are incomplete and OKRs and KRAs miss the mark for businesses like ours, then what's the solution?
Enter the critical accountability bullet. Critical accountability bullets, or CABs for short, are created bottom-up, not top-down. In other words, instead of assigning accountability based on company objectives and even job titles and descriptions, we go back to the value engines, the visual maps of how our company creates and captures value, and we simply ask, who does this? Now this distinction is super important, so let me say it again another way. Most organizations start with a person or role and ask, what does this person do? Or what actions and activities is the person occupying this role supposed to be doing? Then they write out a job description or they list out the key results areas, and they call it done. But while this may be better than nothing, the sequence is all wrong.
You don't start with a person and ask, what does Bob do? You start with the critical tasks and activities that everyone agrees must get done and ask, who does this? If Bob is the person who's supposed to do that task, then great We can assign it to Bob and make sure he knows he's accountable. But the lesson is, we start with what needs to be done and then ask who should do it, not the other way around. This simple sequence change is what distinguishes critical accountability bullets from similar models and frameworks you might have tried in the past. And this sequence change is what's going to allow you to finally have a high output team that stays focused and accountable to the work that matters most.
Okay, now that you know what a critical accountability bullet is and why it works, let's talk about how you assign them. First, you'll need to pull up a value engine and start at the beginning with the very first task or activity and simply ask, who does this? Or to be more specific, you might even ask, who is uniquely responsible for ensuring this critical task is completed? Now, once you have identified the who, you simply add a description of that task in that team member's critical accountability bullet column on the high output team canvas. So for example, let's say we're assigning accountability for this value engine, and we come to this stage. All you have to do is ask, who does this? Let's say at this company, everyone agrees it's Ty Willingham.
Okay, great. Now all we have to do is go back to the high output team canvas and write a description of that stage in Ty Willingham's cab column. Then we move on to the next stage and the next, each time asking, who does this? When you get to a power stage, you might want to get even more granular and ask, you know, who's uniquely responsible for ensuring this critical step is completed, you know, for each and every step of the playbooks that we documented back in that previous module. But you don't have to go to that level of depth if you don't want to. So let's say we come to this stage and we find out that it's actually Jan Jones who's responsible for this particular set of activities.
Again, the process is the same. Just open the high output team canvas, find Jan Jones's name in the corresponding team, and write in a description, a simple bullet point that defines this critical activity. Now, as you can see, we aren't starting with the high output team canvas and asking what does each person do? No, we are starting with the value engines and adding the accountability bullets that are critical. And how do we know what's truly critical and what isn't? Simple. If it's represented as a stage of a value engine or a major step in a power stage, it's critical. In other words, we're allowing our value engines, the visual representations of how we as a company create and capture value, to inform the most important areas of accountability within this.
This literally gets everyone on the same page and aligned to the work that matters most, the work that absolutely must get done, even at the expense of other seemingly urgent but not important activities Once you've gone through all your value engines and assigned accountability to an individual for each and every stage, then and only then should you go back to the high output team canvas and fill in three to five critical activity bullets for the remaining team members. Just ask, okay, what else does this particular person do? That's truly critical, but that we just haven't already listed. You should also share the list of cabs with each member of your team and ask them, hey, what critical tasks and activities are you accountable to that aren't on this list?
In other words, what did we miss? Make it collaborative. But at the end of the day, remember that this is a living document. So it's not like creating it once and then never touching it again because here's the deal. There are truly critical tasks and activities that won't show up on a value engine Some will be related to internal fulfillment engines that haven't been documented yet. Activities like HR, finance, and accounting, and others will relate to value engines that you just haven't gotten around to documenting, but that you know still exist. That's why the goal isn't to only list out the activity bullets that align with the value engine stage. The goal is to prioritize the value engine stages because this is how we define the most important and the most critical work of the company.
Over time, more and more of your cabs will relate directly to a documented value engine. In fact, this process will likely inform the value engines that should probably be documented next, which is kind of how this whole process is supposed to work. Okay. Once you have assigned accountability for all the critical actions and activities, and once you've added other critical accountability bullets to the other members of your team, congratulations. Not only have you built the first version of your high output team canvas, you have finalized the documentation of the set of algorithms that will form your company's operating system. So job well done, but don't stop now. You are making real progress, but there's still a lot more work to do.
